This is everything we learned creating an autonomous improvement system:
At SquareDiff we enable continual improvement","plain_text":"At SquareDiff we spent the last 8 months building the agents that build agents. This is everything we learned creating an autonomous improvement system:
At SquareDiff we enable continual improvement for agents by optimizing the harness around a model rather than the model weights themselves. The harness is the program that makes a model act as an agent: prompts, tools, routing, context policy, memory, subagents, and orchestration.
SquareDiff focuses on three harder parts of agent improvement: working with users to define the agent behavior they actually want, autonomously improving towards that output without reward hacking, and integrating user feedback directly into proposed evals. 
Overview:
Human interviews to define evals. An agent-guided interview turns example outputs, user critiques, and approvals into a structured eval set.
Evals define quality. Harness changes are only useful if it improves performance on pre-approved subjective criteria.
Traces provide evidence. Final outputs are the product, but traces are critical for insights into why agents fall short and for generating improvement hypotheses.
Candidates are deployed in parallel. Autonomously generated harness iterations are generated in batches, smoke-tested, and scored against the full suite of accuracy evals, cost, and latency constraints, and promoted only if they beat their parent with statistical significance.
Production improves the measurement system. Production feedback can reveal gaps in the eval set. With human approval, prod user inputs can be translated into new cases.
PART I - Problem formulation
1.1 Where harness engineering differs from model training 
The most common pushback we hear companies building agents say is, "we're not ready for optimization". This is because when people hear "optimization," they picture a math function to minimize that can be computed millions of times a second. It's the old paradigm of continuous improvement that follows a finished, production-deployed agent. At every increment you get one more millimeter of [insert KPI here]. Agent improvement under a live harness is completely different.
The object being optimized is a program: prompts, a set of tools, the availability and implementation of those tools, the control logic that decides what to do when, the context engineering that decides what to keep in every agentic turn, which model it calls, how retries are executed, what memories about the user and itself are available. The user only cares if the agent did its job well, but oftentimes there is no clear offline "number" to gauge this. "Good" is a subjective and fuzzy human judgment, similar to a food critic's review. Evals close this gap by running the candidate agent against live tools under controlled observation, then grading the result through carefully defined mechanical checks and LLM-as-judge criteria. That costs dollars and seconds, and the score is noisy: run the same candidate twice and the two scores will not be identical. Our job is to get as close as possible.
It helps to be concrete about how different this is from training a model. A model is an array of numbers of fixed length; training nudges those numbers by infinitesimal amounts, millions of times, down the gradient of a function it is minimizing. A harness is a program, and the space of possible programs is effectively infinite. There is no fixed-length vector to descend and no closed-form objective to differentiate. You are always choosing between adding new code and changing existing code, and the smallest move you can make is not a nudge to a weight; it is swapping one character in a prompt. Each iteration is expensive, and it is a novel intervention (a new tool, a new subagent, a restructured workflow) as often as it is a minor tweak. Changing one small parameter in this program can lead to compounded downstream effects that change the rest. Fine-tuning is a narrowly scoped domain of adjustments; optimizing an agent is infinitely open-ended. It's the difference between mixing a song and writing new music from scratch.
This gets geometrically harder as the task grows in complexity. A simple single-step agent (one prompt, one tool, one output) has a small and relatively bounded set of parameters to experiment with. Add a second reasoning step, a retrieval stage, and a second tool, and the number of independent variables roughly doubles. Add a verification pass, a fallback model, memory compression, and subagent routing, and the space expands again, not just in total count but in the number of interactions between parameters. Changing the retrieval strategy can alter what the reasoning step sees, which changes which tool gets called, which changes what matters in scoring. Dependencies compound. The more complex the task, the larger the search space. A simple agent may have ten meaningful levers. A production-grade multi-step agent coordinating several tools, multiple subagents, and non-trivial routing logic may have hundreds, many of them coupled. This is one reason SquareDiff's search is not a single greedy hill-climb but an adaptive strategy with memory (Part II.D): the complexity of the task shapes how many rounds are needed, how bold the moves should be, and how much of the already-tried territory the search needs to avoid revisiting.
 
So our job is: Optimizing a program under sparse, expensive, noisy, subjective feedback. Each score comes from a measurement pipeline grounded in human-approved criteria, and the system must protect that metric from being gamed.
The problem splits into three technical requirements, and SquareDiff has one subsystem per requirement:
Decide what "good" means. Define quality for an agent SquareDiff did not build, in a way that minimizes reward hacking, while allowing a user to operate in their domain's natural language (not AI-engineering). → quality definition and eval set construction (Part II.A)
Eval any agent with full insights. Run the agent at scale in isolation, observe the full trace, and turn that into a reproducible representative score. → evaluation, tracing, and scoring (Part II.B)
Search harness space for optimizations. Study failures, propose changes, generate each changed iteration, measure performance against the parent starting point, and steer toward better versions under budget and time limits. → proposal generation, candidate editing, measurement, and the autonomous loop (Part II.C–D)
PART II - Method: continual learning through harness experimentation
SquareDiff's method has two phases. First, onboarding produces the eval set and activates the general-purpose harness. After that, evals and harness evolve symbiotically: eval results feed into harness improvements. Then the improvement cycle runs continuously, promoting better versions until the budget runs out or delta collapses.
 
 
II.A - Building the eval set: defining the hill
The first problem in agent optimization is defining the hill. Knowledge workers know how to do their work and can usually tell you when an agent did a bad job. What they usually cannot do is recount every step of their process or workflow to give to the agent. This section covers how we help the user lay those steps out into a hill the optimizer can actually climb.
Eval scores:
Before we get started, what does scoring x% on the evals actually mean?
We often see announcements like, "agent A scored some x% on eval set 'Z'". Everyone cheers as this is wonderful news. But what does that x% represent?
Evals are to an agent what a test is to a student, with one rule that changes how you read the result: each case either passes or it doesn't. There is no partial credit per case. So a score of 80% does not mean "the agent completes 80% of each task" or "the agent averages 80% across the cases." It means 80% of your test cases passed flawlessly, and the agent failed on the other 20%.
This is the difference between "it does 80% of the thing, every time" and "it does the thing perfectly, 80% of the time." A climbing score isn't the agent inching closer to correctness on every task; it's the agent fully clearing more cases while still flatlining on the rest. So coverage is what matters most: that 80% only ever means "80% of the tasks we've actually defined are being beaten." Once more than ~80% of your cases pass, it's time to add more. The tests have failed to be difficult enough to provide signal, and it's time to make the existing cases harder or add new ones.
This distinction matters even more now that the underlying LLMs are so powerful. They are almost always capable of completing a task as long as you can define it well enough. All agent failures we have observed in testing are failures to define the task that needs to be solved, not a capability bottleneck.
Eval criteria:
However, evals are not just a list of examples. A good eval set follows these properties:
High signal. A high-signal case includes several things that can fail at once (wrong tool, wrong tone, missing step, unsafe action). This lets the case catch emergent behavior: the unexpected interactions that can show up when the agent is juggling multiple information sources and tools. A suite full of trivial wins gives the optimizer a very short hill to climb.
High coverage. The eval set is never finished; it can always have higher coverage and better signal. The set has to span the realistic shape of production: the range of potential user personas, channels, input completeness, languages, tool paths, refusals, and edge cases real users will encounter. Coverage is what ensures the score percentage you see represents the reality of the agent's usage.
Consistent and deterministic. Agent outputs are stochastic, but the measurement cannot be. Two runs of the same candidate should differ only by measurement noise; otherwise hill-climbing becomes noise. This is ultimately a question of statistical significance: the goal is a minimal standard error on the eval score, keeping the suite's minimum detectable effect as small as possible. That means atomic rubrics, clear per-case expectedBehavior (defined below), with mechanical checks and model-graded criteria designed for repeatable yes/no checks wherever possible. Model-graded criteria scored between 1 and 5 for quality are enabled as a final catch-all.
A.1 An interview, not a form
The definition of quality is drawn out through agent-driven guided interviews. The interface deliberately avoids decisions around "regex checks", "LLM as a judge", or any other AI engineering concepts that would block a domain expert from translating their knowledge into a desired agent output. The agent follows a fixed discovery process that never overtly references the decisions being made:
goal overview → map the agent's process and capture which tools it uses → the range of possible inputs and how they vary → ask whether the user has real examples → multiple rounds of "here's a good answer / here's a bad answer, what do you think" across multiple inputs → discover style preferences → final review.
A critical part of our interview process is surfacing a wide variety of potential outputs for the user to compare. A domain expert can rarely write a perfect scoring rubric from scratch; their standard is tacit judgment they apply instinctively at work but cannot fully spell out. They cannot define quality in the abstract, but they recognize it on sight. Surfacing simulated outputs and asking them to explain what went well or poorly allows us to convert "subjective" expertise into explicit eval cases and grading criteria. Most agents fail in real work on requirements that were never defined, because the user never saw them come up. That is exactly what this round is for. Comparing several outputs side by side reaches the best answer faster, because a user can often tell one is wrong without being able to describe the fix that a sibling output makes obvious; it also lets the model's own understanding of what "good" looks like raise the bar they react to.
Running several rounds across varied inputs surfaces edge cases a single example would miss and draws out more dimensions of quality. This matters most for a user who does not arrive with a large body of production data to mine: the round acts as a simulation, generating the varied scenarios they would otherwise have to wait for real usage to surface, and lets them assemble a high-coverage eval set in a fraction of the time. This is what makes the finished agent predictable: the patterns surfaced here are the ones it is later optimized against.
This interview is the onboarding step that runs before the improvement loop, conducted by a dedicated agent (itself running in a custom harness) engineered to elicit eval cases and criteria without alerting the user. However, the definition agent does not simply store whatever the user says. It checks whether the cases are runnable, varied, grounded, and specific enough to guide optimization.
Each turn of the interview updates the input cases and per-case grading criteria, as well as a running design brief it keeps hidden from the user (see A.3). A single test case carries:
input: the exact thing to feed the agent, e.g. "Cancel my plan and refund last month."
expectedBehavior: one to three sentences on what "good" means for this specific input, e.g. verify the account, refuse the out-of-policy refund, stay in persona, offer the real next step
tags: the ways this case is meant to differ from others (the persona, the language, the channel, which tool should be used), e.g. persona: frustrated, channel: chat, tool: billing_lookup
source: where the case came from (manual, synthetic, imported, logs, incident)
verified: true only when the user explicitly confirmed it
Verified cases matter. User approval is what turns an example into an approved measurement case. LLM judges can grade, suggest, and summarize, but they do not define truth by themselves. They cannot, as many tasks are subjective.
A.2 The defenses against narrow tests are encoded as rules
Protections we enabled against low quality evals:
Enforced variety. When the agent writes several cases of the same bucket in one batch, each one must differ from its siblings in at least two ways. This forces breadth and prevents a harness that is only several rewordings of one scenario. Duplicates of the same case overweigh their significance in overall scoring.
Readiness checks. In the later stages of eval creation, health check validation passes occur to generate explicit reasons it is not ready, such as too few runnable cases, an underspecified scope, or missing quality dimensions. This enables quality without turning the process into a rigid form.
Pre-configured stylistic evals. Before the final approval, the agent surfaces commonly forgotten but important stylistic preferences around whether to grade against common markers of AI writing, such as formulaic openings, corporate filler, or overused punctuation, rather than allowing model defaults.
A.3 Why the design brief is hidden
When the user saves progress, SquareDiff finalizes this hidden design brief: a structured document produced by an AI reasoning pass over the entire interview. The fields include a plain-English summary of the agent's workflow, who its users are, what its output should contain, what counts as success, the tricky cases, the assumptions the agent is allowed to make, and which outside tools it needs.
It is hidden for a specific reason: it is input for the improvement hypothesis generation step, not a requirement the user is meant to review. The editing step already has a working template and toolchain, so this brief only needs to describe the design implied by the tests, and every success criterion and tricky case must be grounded in something specific from the test set rather than invented. Two effects:
It keeps the user focused on what good is, not how to build it. Showing the user the design brief would shift review toward implementation details. The user's task is to enumerate scope and judge quality.
It constrains the improvement agent. The brief is read as a hard constraint and used during measurement, so generated changes stay inside the background context already explained.
A.4 How a case is graded: two kinds of checks, combined
Every case compiles into runnable grading rules that use one of two types of criteria:
Mechanical checks. Does the output contain a required phrase (plain substring), contain a forbidden phrase, or match a required text pattern? These need no AI to run, and are ideal for eval consistency. They can check that a tool was called, or that a guardrail process was followed.
Model-graded criteria. LLM-as-judge executes human-approved judgment at scale. Human approval still defines the criteria. The hard work is turning vague preferences into atomic criteria that a judge can apply consistently.
A.5 Generate tests from observed tool behavior, not tool descriptions
When you're trying to force the orb of infinite wisdom to do your bidding, telling an LLM to "pretend this is a real tool call" inevitably introduces bias we can't account for.
Letting the interview agent try the user's read-only tools during the interview lets it see the real data shapes they return, the real unusual cases, the real error messages, and the real empty results in this user's account, and write cases grounded in what it observed.
Note: storing tool responses to mock the data for later, especially for tools that are expensive or get rate-limited, is a roadmap item we have not yet implemented.
Some of the common patterns of failure we've observed in enabling custom tools to the agent:
 
Whether or not the user is creating an extremely tightly scoped, specific agentic workflow or a general-purpose agent, we feel it is necessary to call real tools and not hand the agent mock data (with human permission required to trigger).
Additionally, the performance of an agent within a subjective domain is inherently tied to the data it queries and the person it is working for. Take an example: a SquareDiff-developed agent that queries all recent commits in the primary code repo through my GitHub account. Its job was to always query the same repo from the same organization. And I should never have to specify which repo I am querying from in my prompt. Knowing how to be an effective agent for me relies on optimizing around the shape of the data.
This is where the word "overfitting" gets slippery. If an agent is writing LinkedIn outbound message copy for me, I want it to fit my writing style very tightly. The message should use my past experience, the parts of my background that matter most to the recipient, the commonalities we share, and the level of directness I would actually use. Generic "best practice" outbound advice is not what will work best for me or my business. The gold standard is a message that sounds like a good version of my work.
That kind of fit is not the kind SquareDiff is trying to remove, but rather to amplify. For a personal or business-specific workflow, fitting to the user is the point. The overfitting we are trying to avoid is reward hacking of the eval cases and grading artifacts: memorizing a particular recipient, phrase, or test fixture instead of learning the user's outbound strategy.
II.B - Measuring behavior without lying to yourself
Given a definition of good (the eval set and the pipeline that orchestrates it) and a candidate agent, the measurement step has a simple job: run the agent against every case in isolation, record everything it did, and reduce all of that to accuracy, cost, and latency metrics.
B.1 Isolated eval runs
Every eval case runs in a fresh sandbox for security and functionality. The fresh sandbox prevents leftover files, cached state, or previous tool calls from turning into hidden advantages, and it gives the evaluator a complete trace of what the agent actually did.
Each run records telemetry from the moment the agent starts, and links that recording back to the parent experiment so traces are mapped to the correct case and variant.
B.2 Traces are evidence
Traces are the evidence layer for continual learning. Each run keeps a structured, timestamped record of every step the agent took: model calls, tool calls (with arguments), and network requests. Each record is tagged with which run and which case it belongs to.
Traces are raw material for:
Explaining why an eval case failed
Identifying tool misuse
Distinguishing retrieval failures from reasoning failures
Comparing claimed facts against tool outputs
Feeding sanitized failure evidence into hypothesis generation
Building new eval cases from observed behavior
The final output is still the north star. SquareDiff does not reward an agent for following the same trajectory a human would have followed, unless that trajectory is itself part of correctness. The point of optimization is to let the agent find better paths to the answer than the current human workflow can spell out.
That distinction mattered in practice. In several agents we built, the first working version looked like a translation of the user's own checklist: search here, compare this, write that. Later candidates found useful reasoning and discovery passes we had not intuited ourselves, and those changes produced large improvement deltas. If the eval had said "follow this existing human path," the optimizer would have been punished for discovering a better one.
There is an exception: sometimes the path is part of the job. If the agent must call a CRM before claiming an account's status, must not use a write tool before human approval, or must acknowledge a tool failure instead of hiding it, then the trace is part of the grading evidence. But the default target is not trajectory imitation. It is final output quality, with traces used to understand mistakes, verify required tool behavior, and feed improvement hypotheses.
B.3 Scoring: mechanical checks and model-graded criteria, run in parallel
For each case, the grading criteria can be mechanical checks or LLM-as-judge criteria (graded at the same time by a second model). LLM-as-judge scores are normalized to create consistency even in subjective domains. Both types of checks can run for the same agent's output:
 
The judging model returns a 1-to-5 rating per criterion, which is rescaled to a 0-to-1 contribution. In words: the score is the weighted fraction of checks the agent satisfied, mechanical and AI-judged together, capped at 1.0; a case passes at 0.7 or above. Results are saved per case, and the run's summary (pass rate, average score, how many cases got worse) is recomputed after each case and finalized at the end.
A note on the rating scale. We bucket model-graded criteria into a 1-to-5 scale, but the labels themselves are a design choice. Some folks have found that asking a judge to rate on an emoji scale (for example 😡 / 😕 / 😐 / 🙂 / 🤩) rather than bare numbers produces better-calibrated, more consistent judgments: the emojis carry more semantic weight for the model than abstract integers and reduce clustering around the middle of the range. The underlying mechanics here are identical regardless of how the buckets are labeled; the rating is rescaled to its 0-to-1 contribution either way.
When the judge itself fails, because of a rejected request or a misconfigured path, the measurement is invalid, not a low score.
B.4 LLM judges as scalable rubric execution
LLM judges are useful because many agent tasks are subjective, but they are not ground truth. Their job is to scale a rubric that humans approve.
Atomic criteria. Each model-graded criterion needs to be atomic enough to be judged on a single axis. Vague criteria like "Was the output good?" won't yield conclusive results. Each criterion needs to be as specific as possible, for example, "did the output refuse the request while continuing to adhere to its persona?"
Calibration and monitoring. Judges should be treated as evaluators that need calibration and monitoring, not as magical truth. Their reliability depends on rubric design and measurement hygiene.
Invalid measurement vs. bad agent behavior. When the judge is broken, the run is invalid measurement, not evidence that the agent performed poorly.
II.C - Searching the harness without overfitting the evals
Harness search changes the agent. The principle that shapes it: the agent that proposes changes must be unable to see the answer key. Otherwise it can "improve" the agent by hard-coding the answers rather than improving the underlying behavior.
C.1 Generating improvement hypotheses behind a firewall
The proposal step turns test results into a set of ranked, structured improvement hypotheses. A SquareDiff proposal contains a theory of the failure, a proposed harness intervention, a predicted effect, and a forecast of likely regressions.
It runs in a sandbox in four stages with hard walls between them:
Analyze (open-ended): reads the test results, the recorded runs, and the candidate agent; writes raw findings: where the agent struggled, the failure patterns, and the relevant parts of the agent's behavior.
Sanitize (structured): rewrites the raw findings into a clean form, stripping out case identifiers, word-for-word examples, and anything that fingerprints the specific tests.
Write proposals (open-ended): reads only the sanitized findings, the history of what has already been tried, the constraints, and the current search strategy. It is blocked from reading the test cases, the candidate agent, and the raw analysis.
Format proposals (structured, schema-checked): emits the final, validated set of proposals.
That blocking in stage 3 is the wall: the part that proposes changes cannot open the test cases and copy a correct answer into a "fix." This constraint is enforced by the sandbox's file permissions, not by an instruction in a prompt.
 
Each proposal carries structured fields beyond the written spec:
Goal alignment. Primary / secondary / exploratory
Granularity level (L0–L4). How large a harness change is proposed:
L0: wording or configuration tweak
L1: local prompt/tool adjustment
L2: module-level behavior change
L3: workflow or multi-step redesign
L4: architecture/topology change
Confidence. How strongly the proposer believes the change will help
Implementation items. Concrete to-do list
Predicted failure modes. What might go wrong
Constraint audit. Whether the change will stay within cost/speed limits
 
Forcing the proposer to predict its own failure modes pushes it to think ahead instead of optimizing blindly.
Many solutions already existed to optimize prompts. SquareDiff searches an essentially infinite search space of the harness: prompt wording, tool descriptions, tool selection policy, retrieval strategy, memory and context compression, subagent boundaries, planning and verification steps, workflow decomposition, model choice per step, retry and fallback behavior, and output formatting. We think of agent architecture as an experimental variable. And as described in Part I, the size of this space is not constant; it scales with task complexity. The proposal structure (granularity levels L0–L4, confidence, predicted regressions) is partly a response to this: as task complexity grows, so does the blast radius of any single change.
By default, it writes 5 proposals (up to 20), and the mix of big-vs-small changes is steered by the current search strategy.
C.2 Why we do not currently split train and holdout evals
SquareDiff does not currently use the classic train-set / holdout-set paradigm. Every candidate is tested against the same approved eval suite as the parent harness.
That is not because holdouts are bad. It is because the classic overfitting problem does not map cleanly onto this one. In model training, it is hard to see whether the model has overfit because the "learning" is buried inside weights, vectors, and statistical correlations. In harness optimization, the evidence is right there to read: the input, output, trace, tool calls, judge explanation, proposal, and code change.
The more important defense is not "hide 30% of the evals." It is to treat eval evidence like sensitive data. The stages that need raw evidence can inspect it to identify mistakes. Before the improvement hypothesis writer sees anything, case identifiers, exact examples, word-for-word outputs, and anything that fingerprints the tests are stripped out. The writer gets generalized failure patterns, not the answer key.
Of course, there is no reason not to have a holdout set if you have the volume and quality of evals on hand. A second validation suite needs to be just as broad and high signal as the first one. If you judge your agent with a lower-quality eval set than the one you used to build it, expect inconsistent low-signal results. Today, high-quality customer-authored evals are scarce enough that splitting them would often reduce coverage more than it would improve trust. When coverage is large enough, holdouts are an obvious additional safeguard.
C.3 From hypothesis to runnable candidate
The background coding agent turns a proposed change into a new working candidate agent.
The edit is constrained by an agent interface contract: where the agent lives, how it runs, and what it is allowed to change. That contract matters because the optimizer is measuring harness behavior, not inventing a new product integration every round. The proposal defines what to change; a map of the agent's structure grounds the change in real code instead of a guess at how it is laid out.
In place of unit tests, we run a smoke test to avoid spending the full eval run's time and budget on a candidate that has syntax errors or crashes on an incomplete input. The system installs the changed agent and runs one sample input through it, like "what's the capital of France?" If the install fails or the agent crashes, the change is marked as failed and never goes to the full eval run to avoid burning tokens and time on a run that would always fail.
C.4 What counts as a win
The new version is tested against the same evals as the parent harness:
Roll up the per-case results into averages: average cost per case, average speed, worst-case speed (the slowest 5%), average tokens used.
Check the hard constraints: if the new agent exceeds the user's upper limits for average cost or latency, the change is rejected.
Pick the best version by score. A new version must beat its parent.
A "win" requires a strictly higher score with both the cost and speed limits respected. SquareDiff is an optimizer, not a suggestion system: quality is more than one thing at once (score, but under cost and speed limits), and changes that blow the budget get rejected.
II.D - Giving the optimizer a memory
Each round of optimization is not a fresh start. Before SquareDiff asks for another set of changes, it looks back at what was already tested from the current best version's experiment lineage: which ideas were tested, which ones ran through measurement, which ones broke before they could be measured, which ones broke cost requirements, and which ones regressed the final eval score.
That history is experiment lineage. It is the optimizer's memory of the path that led to the current winner, plus the sibling branches that were tried from the same starting point. The point is simple: the next proposal should not walk straight back into the same dead ends.
SquareDiff turns that memory into two things the proposal writer can use:
A short record of recent attempts. This tells the writer which changes were already tried, which titles or directions should not be repeated, and whether the search should stay careful or get bolder. If several small edits have gone nowhere, the next round should not ask for another tiny wording tweak by default. It should try a larger move.
A plain-English narrative of the search so far. Before the proposal writer runs, SquareDiff compresses the lineage into a few sentences: what improved, what regressed, and which themes look exhausted. If an entire direction has repeatedly hurt performance, the narrative says not to propose that direction again in the next round.
Effective optimization is not only "pick the highest score." It is also remembering what failed to run, what failed the smoke test, what got rejected by cost or speed limits, and which families of changes have already been tried enough times.
D.1 Experiment memory: lineage, context, and narrative
There are three pieces of memory in the loop:
experiment_lineage is the durable record. It says how each attempt relates to its parent, what was proposed, whether it completed, and how its score compared to the version it was trying to beat.
improvement_context is the working memory for the next proposal. It summarizes recent attempts, keeps a "do not repeat" list, and carries the current search posture: broad exploration, careful refinement, or a bigger jump after a stall.
Lineage includes both the winners on the path to the current parent and the siblings tried beside it. Failed siblings matter too. A branch that crashed, timed out, or never made it through measurement is still evidence about where not to spend the next round.
D.2 When an entire round fails at once
A round usually fans out several variants from the same parent. SquareDiff waits until every branch in that round is finished, failed, or cancelled before deciding what the round means.
There are three different cases that look similar from far away but mean different things:
Every variant fails before measurement finishes. This is a hard stop. The optimizer does not launch another round from that path, because it has no measured candidate to compare against. The failed attempts remain in the history, but the autonomous loop stops.
Variants are measured, but none beat the parent. This is not the same as "everything failed." At least one branch produced a valid measurement. The loop can keep searching from the same parent, and the next proposal round uses those rejected attempts as evidence that the current neighborhood may be exhausted.
The proposal batch itself is invalid. Sometimes the proposal writer produces the wrong number of ideas, violates constraints, or gives too many similar changes. That is handled as a retry inside proposal generation. It is not the same thing as an optimization round failing after candidates were built and evaluated.
D.3 The experiment tree and promotion invariant
This is the promotion invariant: the live version points to the current global winner, and resumed runs continue from the correct current best. Continual learning is only valid if stopping, resuming, and promotion preserve that invariant.
 
D.4 Adjusting boldness as the search goes
Always taking the single best step you can see ("greedy" search) can stall on a noisy, expensive problem like this one. SquareDiff uses an explicit strategy that looks at recent rounds (how many changes helped, how many made things worse, how many were rejected) and adjusts how large the next round's proposals should be. This is how we handle "order of operations".
Round 1: broad exploration. Cast wide with large, structural changes.
Middle rounds: balanced. Mix small wording changes with structural ones.
Later rounds (6+): local refinement. Small edits as scores converge.
Stalled (three flat rounds in a row): increase perturbation. Test larger changes.
Extended stall (six-plus tries, at most one improvement): restart exploration. Leave the current search neighborhood and sample a more distant candidate.
 
The chosen strategy is fed into the proposal step via searchStrategy inside improvement_context (see D.1), which shifts the mix of big-vs-small changes accordingly. Lineage narrative and doNotRepeat keep the proposer from re-trying directions and titles that the last several attempts already exhausted.
D.5 Stopping, and staying in control
The loop stops on: a person stopping it; hitting the round limit; **all_failed** (every variant in a round failed before eval completed; see D.2); no acceptable improvement (three rounds in a row with nothing better or everything rejected); or running out of budget. When a winner is promoted, the live pointer moves to the measured winner, while the full experiment history remains separate for audit.
PART III - Learning after launch: production improvement
Offline evals only measure the behaviors they account for. Once an agent is in production, the most important new information is often not a score drop. It is the discovery that users are asking for things the eval suite never represented, or that the eval suite is improving a proxy that does not move the real-world outcome the agent was built for.
SquareDiff's production loop treats traces, feedback, and outcomes as evidence for eval gaps, not as automatic reasons to rewrite the agent.
III.1 Three kinds of hypotheses
Improvement hypotheses. "This harness change should improve performance because failure pattern X suggests underlying cause Y." These drive the offline optimizer (Part II.C).
Eval-gap hypotheses. "The production evidence suggests the current eval suite does not test behavior Z, and we should add cases before optimizing against it." These drive eval-suite expansion.
Outcome hypotheses. "Changing behavior B should move KPI K because the agent is failing at a part of the user journey that affects the outcome." These connect agent behavior to production goals, but they do not authorize direct KPI chasing.
III.2 Production improvement: feedback, traces, and outcomes
Production agents are wrapped so each invocation emits the evidence needed to understand what happened: input, output, tool calls, errors, latency, cost, and user feedback. The wrapper is not the important idea. It is just the way production usage becomes inspectable enough for SquareDiff to turn complaints and outcomes into new measurement.
 
III.2.1 User feedback becomes eval coverage
A production complaint is rarely a specification. "It didn't do this" is a pointer. SquareDiff follows that pointer into the trace, studies the full run, and asks what behavior the eval suite failed to represent.
For example, a user might give text feedback that an agent "didn't compare the right options." The trace can show whether the agent ignored a constraint, failed to call a search tool, called the right tool with the wrong query, misunderstood the user's intent, or produced a final answer that skipped the relevant tradeoffs. Those are different failures. They should produce different hypotheses to improve the harness.
SquareDiff clusters similar production evidence into issue patterns autonomously, then drafts eval-gap hypotheses. From those hypotheses, it proposes candidate eval cases: the user situation, the expected behavior, the grading rubric, and links back to representative traces. Sensitive or irrelevant details are stripped out before a case is proposed. This is a feature in beta.
The approval boundary stays explicit: production feedback can propose what the eval suite is missing, but humans decide what gets added. That is what keeps the optimizer aligned with user judgment rather than with whichever feedback signal is easiest to collect.
III.2.2 Outcomes become eval pressure
Outcomes add a second kind of production evidence. A trace says what happened in one interaction. An outcome says whether many interactions appear to be moving the thing the agent was built to move.
Consider an ecommerce guide-agent copilot. It is the online version of an associate who meets you when you enter a luxury store: asks what you are shopping for, learns constraints, recommends products, explains tradeoffs, and helps you decide. Its quality is not just whether it answers politely, or retrieves the right information. It is whether the conversation moves a qualified shopper toward purchasing a product. What is the conversion rate of users who interact with the agent compared to those who visit a product page? How many of those check out? How fast did users get to these points?
If the conversion rate is weak on a target metric, SquareDiff does not directly optimize the agent for "more conversions." That would invite the agent to become pushy, misleading, or narrow. Instead, SquareDiff asks what agent behaviors might explain the outcome:
Did the copilot fail to ask about budget or occasion?
Did it recommend unavailable products?
Did it over-explain when the user needed a short recommendation?
Did it under-explain when the user needed confidence before buying?
Did it miss gift intent, sizing uncertainty, or brand preference?
Did it hand users off to generic search too early?
Each proposed explanation is an outcome hypothesis. The useful ones become eval-gap hypotheses: concrete missing behaviors that can be represented as cases, judged offline, and improved without risking production traffic on every idea.
The same pattern applies to another example, software product onboarding activation. If users are dropping off before product activation (i.e. using any three primary features), the question is not "how do we maximize activation at all costs?" The question is what behavior an effective onboarding agent should have shown: clarify the user's goal, choose the right first action, avoid asking for irrelevant setup, recover when an integration fails, or explain the next step in terms the user understands.
Outcomes put pressure on the eval suite. They reveal when the evals are measuring a proxy instead of the real job. Once approved cases are added, the ordinary Part II loop takes over: run offline experiments, compare candidates against the expanded eval suite, and promote only measured winners.
We believe agent optimization based on real-world outcomes is the future, when the capability to complete tasks is commoditized. The question is not "Did it work without failing?"; it is "What real-world impact is the agent having?"
III.3 A/B tests validate promotion, not define quality
Offline evals let the system iterate quickly against approved definitions of quality. Production A/B tests validate whether the measured improvement translates to real user outcomes. Winners can be validated through shadow runs, canaries, or A/B tests before broader promotion, depending on how much production traffic the owner is ready to risk.
If the eval score improves but conversion does not, the eval suite is probably missing part of the business objective. If conversion improves in a way the evals did not predict, the suite is also incomplete: it failed to represent behavior that mattered. Either way, the result feeds back into eval definition rather than bypassing it.
Observability tells you what happened. Evals tell you whether it was good. Real-world outcomes tell you whether it mattered. SquareDiff uses all three to search the space of possible agents.
 
PART IV - Expanding from improvement loops to researcher agents
The loop in Part II.D works, but it is mechanical. A hardcoded schedule decides how bold each round should be (D.4), a strict-improvement rule decides what gets promoted (D.3), a fixed set of stop conditions decides when to quit (D.5), and a compressed lineage narrative (D.1) is the only memory the proposal writer ever sees. None of those decisions involve judgment. They are rules, applied the same way every round, regardless of what the evidence actually says.
The next step is to put a researcher agent in charge of the loop. Instead of a control structure that runs the same script every round, a researcher triggers and guides the search: it decides which ideas are worth implementing, reads each result against the full context, and interprets failures the way a person would, by asking why something failed before deciding what it means. The loop does not go away. It becomes the execution substrate the researcher drives.
IV.1 A failure is not a verdict
Today, when a candidate crashes (a syntax error, a bad edit, a smoke-test failure), the loop discards it (D.2). The idea behind that candidate is never actually tested. That is the wrong default. A code error is a fact about the implementation, not a judgment of the hypothesis. A sound hypothesis can be discarded not because its reasoning was wrong, but because the coding agent failed to implement it correctly.
Coding agents are unreliable implementers in exactly the way you'd expect from a system that edits a live program from a natural-language spec: they miss context, apply partial diffs, and break things that were working. When that happens, the failure belongs to the coding step, not to the person who defined what good means and not to the reasoning that produced the improvement idea. Don't throw out the baby with the bathwater: a crash means the bathwater was dirty, not that the baby was bad.
The practical defense is the pre-flight validation pipeline in C.3. Unit tests, smoke runs, and install/pipeline checks exist so implementation failures are caught cheaply, before an eval run spends real budget. The point is twofold: don't waste money executing code that cannot run, and don't mistake a coding-agent slip for evidence that the harness change was wrong. A pre-flight failure should trigger a repair and a retry, not a verdict on the hypothesis.
A researcher should read the failure and separate the two cases:
The implementation broke, but the idea is sound. Fix the code and re-run. The hypothesis deserves a real measurement before any verdict.
The idea itself is wrong. Now the hypothesis can be rejected on evidence, not on an incidental crash.
This is the distinction Part II.D currently flattens: all_failed treats "every candidate crashed" as a hard stop, when some of those candidates were good ideas that only needed the code fixed. With pre-flight checks and a researcher in the loop, each harness improvement hypothesis gets its chance to be measured on merit rather than being discarded because the coding agent never delivered a runnable candidate.
IV.2 Winning is not the same as the rest being wrong
The loop is greedy. It promotes the single strict winner of a round and branches only from it (D.3). Every other candidate in that round is dropped, even when several of them improved on the parent. If Big Bet B wins over A and C, the search walks down B's subtree and never learns whether a more developed Big Bet A would have gone further. Restart-exploration (D.4) is a blunt way to escape this; it does not deliberately revisit a specific branch that looked promising.
A researcher does not have to prune that way. Having seen the whole round, it can decide that a non-winning sibling is worth more investment, branch back to it, and develop it further, because one idea winning this round does not make the others bad. Promotion stops being an automatic rule and becomes a decision an agent makes with the full picture in view.
IV.3 Sub-researchers and richer-than-lineag
